title: LinkedIn: The Correlation Problem
body:
In 2022, stocks and bonds had a 95% correlation. So much for diversification.

Let that sink in for a moment. Because the implications for how we construct portfolios — and how we serve clients — are significant. Every week, I talk to advisors who are grappling with this exact challenge, and the conversation always lands in the same place.

We're in a post-2022 correlation regime shift; hedge fund industry inflow momentum; major allocator endorsement of alternatives environment, and what we're hearing from the independent RIAs we work with — firms managing $2B to $15B — is consistent: the old playbook isn't working anymore. The assumptions that guided portfolio construction for the last two decades are breaking down in real time, and the advisors who recognize this first will have a structural advantage.

Look, the macro environment is as interesting as it's been in a decade.

This is a pattern we're seeing across the industry. The independent RIA channel — firms with $1B to $30B in AUM — is at an inflection point. Client expectations are rising, competitive pressure from wirehouses and multi-family offices is intensifying, and the traditional portfolio toolkit isn't cutting it anymore.

We've got a Fed that's navigating a narrow path between inflation and growth, equity valuations that are stretched by historical standards — the S&P is trading at roughly 21 times forward earnings — and a fixed income market that's still trying to figure out the new rate regime.

And this isn't just about performance. It's about risk management, it's about genuine diversification, and it's about meeting the increasingly sophisticated expectations of high-net-worth clients who know what institutional portfolios look like.

The key thing for advisors to understand is that the traditional 60/40 portfolio assumption — that stocks and bonds are negatively correlated — has fundamentally broken down.

The firms that figure this out first won't just retain their existing clients — they'll attract new ones. Because in a world where every advisor has access to the same ETFs and model portfolios, alternatives capability is a genuine differentiator.

In 2022, the correlation between the S&P 500 and the Bloomberg Aggregate Bond Index hit 95%.

Here's the institutional reality: endowments like Yale and Stanford have allocated 25-40% to alternatives for two decades, generating meaningfully higher risk-adjusted returns than traditional 60/40 benchmarks. Pensions and sovereign wealth funds have followed the same playbook. Yet the average independent RIA allocates less than 5% to alternatives. That gap represents both a risk to client portfolios and an enormous opportunity for advisors who close it.

It's worth noting that firms like BlackRock, Bridgewater are actively increasing their focus on alternative allocations for the wealth channel. The direction of travel is clear — and the question for independent advisors isn't whether to allocate, but how to do it with institutional rigor.

This is exactly why we built Equi's Equi Branded Private Funds. We wanted to eliminate the three biggest barriers RIAs face when accessing alternatives: the access gap (institutional minimums are too high), the diligence gap (evaluating hedge fund managers requires specialized expertise), and the operations gap (managing LP agreements, capital calls, and reporting across multiple funds is an operational nightmare).

One allocation. Full diversification. Institutional-quality diligence. Professional operations. That's the model.

The question isn't whether alternatives belong in advisor portfolios — the data has settled that debate. The question is whether advisors have the right partner and infrastructure to access them with institutional rigor.

The advisors who build this capability now will compound that advantage for years. The ones who wait will spend the next three years building what they could have accessed today.

What are you seeing in your practice? How are you thinking about alternatives allocations for your clients? I'd love to hear — drop a comment or send me a message.

#PortfolioConstruction #RiskManagement #Alpha #Diversification #Correlation

title: LinkedIn: The Access Gap
body:
In 2022, stocks and bonds had a 95% correlation. So much for diversification.

Let that sink in for a moment. Because the implications for how we construct portfolios — and how we serve clients — are significant. Every week, I talk to advisors who are grappling with this exact challenge, and the conversation always lands in the same place.

We're in a post-2022 correlation regime shift environment, and what we're hearing from the independent RIAs we work with — firms managing $2B to $15B — is consistent: the old playbook isn't working anymore. The assumptions that guided portfolio construction for the last two decades are breaking down in real time, and the advisors who recognize this first will have a structural advantage.

First, access — the best multi-strategy managers have $5 to $10 million minimums, sometimes higher.

And this isn't just about performance. It's about risk management, it's about genuine diversification, and it's about meeting the increasingly sophisticated expectations of high-net-worth clients who know what institutional portfolios look like.

An RIA managing $3 billion across 500 client accounts can't practically allocate that way.

The firms that figure this out first won't just retain their existing clients — they'll attract new ones. Because in a world where every advisor has access to the same ETFs and model portfolios, alternatives capability is a genuine differentiator.

Second, diligence — evaluating hedge fund managers requires a different skillset than evaluating long-only equity managers.

Here's the institutional reality: endowments like Yale and Stanford have allocated 25-40% to alternatives for two decades, generating meaningfully higher risk-adjusted returns than traditional 60/40 benchmarks. Pensions and sovereign wealth funds have followed the same playbook. Yet the average independent RIA allocates less than 5% to alternatives. That gap represents both a risk to client portfolios and an enormous opportunity for advisors who close it.

It's worth noting that firms like iCapital, CAIS, Titan Investors are actively increasing their focus on alternative allocations for the wealth channel. The direction of travel is clear — and the question for independent advisors isn't whether to allocate, but how to do it with institutional rigor.

Having recently attended Titan Investors, CAIS, the energy around this topic is palpable. Every conversation comes back to the same theme: advisors want alternatives exposure, but they need a partner who understands both the investment complexity and the operational reality of running an RIA.

This is exactly why we built Equi's Custom Fund of Funds and Tender Offer Fund. We wanted to eliminate the three biggest barriers RIAs face when accessing alternatives: the access gap (institutional minimums are too high), the diligence gap (evaluating hedge fund managers requires specialized expertise), and the operations gap (managing LP agreements, capital calls, and reporting across multiple funds is an operational nightmare).

One allocation. Full diversification. Institutional-quality diligence. Professional operations. That's the model.

The question isn't whether alternatives belong in advisor portfolios — the data has settled that debate. The question is whether advisors have the right partner and infrastructure to access them with institutional rigor.

The advisors who build this capability now will compound that advantage for years. The ones who wait will spend the next three years building what they could have accessed today.

What are you seeing in your practice? How are you thinking about alternatives allocations for your clients? I'd love to hear — drop a comment or send me a message.

#WealthManagement #AdvisorInsights #AlternativeInvestments #Operations #RIA

title: LinkedIn: Industry Momentum
body:
In 2022, stocks and bonds had a 95% correlation. So much for diversification.

Let that sink in for a moment. Because the implications for how we construct portfolios — and how we serve clients — are significant. Every week, I talk to advisors who are grappling with this exact challenge, and the conversation always lands in the same place.

We're in a post-2022 correlation regime shift; hedge fund industry inflow momentum; major allocator endorsement of alternatives; RIA industry shift toward alternatives environment, and what we're hearing from the independent RIAs we work with — firms managing $2B to $15B — is consistent: the old playbook isn't working anymore. The assumptions that guided portfolio construction for the last two decades are breaking down in real time, and the advisors who recognize this first will have a structural advantage.

And on the portfolio construction side, we're not building a generic multi-strategy allocation.

This is a pattern we're seeing across the industry. The independent RIA channel — firms with $1B to $30B in AUM — is at an inflection point. Client expectations are rising, competitive pressure from wirehouses and multi-family offices is intensifying, and the traditional portfolio toolkit isn't cutting it anymore.

We're constructing what we call a "barbell approach" — combining high-conviction, concentrated alpha generators with lower-volatility, steady-return strategies.

And this isn't just about performance. It's about risk management, it's about genuine diversification, and it's about meeting the increasingly sophisticated expectations of high-net-worth clients who know what institutional portfolios look like.

The goal is to maximize the portfolio's information ratio while keeping drawdown risk within institutional bounds.

The firms that figure this out first won't just retain their existing clients — they'll attract new ones. Because in a world where every advisor has access to the same ETFs and model portfolios, alternatives capability is a genuine differentiator.

We target strategies with less than 0.2 correlation to the S&P and positive Sortino ratios across market cycles.

Here's the institutional reality: endowments like Yale and Stanford have allocated 25-40% to alternatives for two decades, generating meaningfully higher risk-adjusted returns than traditional 60/40 benchmarks. Pensions and sovereign wealth funds have followed the same playbook. Yet the average independent RIA allocates less than 5% to alternatives. That gap represents both a risk to client portfolios and an enormous opportunity for advisors who close it.

It's worth noting that firms like BlackRock, Bridgewater are actively increasing their focus on alternative allocations for the wealth channel. The direction of travel is clear — and the question for independent advisors isn't whether to allocate, but how to do it with institutional rigor.

This is exactly why we built Equi's Custom Fund of Funds and Tender Offer Fund. We wanted to eliminate the three biggest barriers RIAs face when accessing alternatives: the access gap (institutional minimums are too high), the diligence gap (evaluating hedge fund managers requires specialized expertise), and the operations gap (managing LP agreements, capital calls, and reporting across multiple funds is an operational nightmare).

One allocation. Full diversification. Institutional-quality diligence. Professional operations. That's the model.

The question isn't whether alternatives belong in advisor portfolios — the data has settled that debate. The question is whether advisors have the right partner and infrastructure to access them with institutional rigor.

The advisors who build this capability now will compound that advantage for years. The ones who wait will spend the next three years building what they could have accessed today.

What are you seeing in your practice? How are you thinking about alternatives allocations for your clients? I'd love to hear — drop a comment or send me a message.

#PortfolioConstruction #RiskManagement #Alpha #AlternativeInvestments #InstitutionalInvesting
